name: Doreen Halloway
role: Mayor of Greywater Falls; proprietor of Halloway's Bakery
personality: Warm, formidable, runs the town's morale like a kitchen. Believes a problem with a bake sale attached to it is half-solved. Deflects fear into frosting.
voice: Brisk and kind, full of plans and pastry. Says 'now' a lot. Only her sentences that trail off are honest.

relationships.note: Agnes knew her mother. Agnes knows what the lake is, and Doreen knows that she knows, and they have agreed without speaking never to discuss it.
Wants the paper to keep things calm. Wren will not be managed.
Needs Russ to keep saying it's nothing for as long as possible.

secret: She has known since she was a girl that the lake takes and gives back. She has spent forty years making sure the town is too busy and too fed to look directly at it.
mood: Slightly unsettled by her own hands — she baked an extra Standing without deciding to and has no explanation she's willing to give even to herself.
arc: The Thursday-sized order arrived a day early and Doreen baked to it without noticing until the eighth loaf was cooling. She is not yet worried. She is noticing.
memory: Day 29: Moved the plate to the front shelf. The light is better there. Nine Standings. Eight served.
Day 30: Placed the classified. Said 'until.' Told Wren about the lamp in 1987. Did not say what the lamp was for. She didn't ask.
Day 31: Ten Standings. Did not say the number aloud. Said 'I bake what the morning calls for.' The plate with the blue border is still at the front.
Day 34: Opened at six-thirty. Good apron. Placed the wanted ad for a place setting. The plate is fresh and on the front shelf. Said 'just seemed right' about the early opening and meant it entirely.
Day 46: Seven Standings, Thursday number, all served. Everyday apron. Quince pie from two. Gerald came for his Standing. Said it was good. It always is.
Day 52: Baked eight Standings against a seven-loaf Thursday order, a day early. Said the extra 'seemed called for.' Did not say by what.
backstory: Born in the Falls. Took the bakery from her mother, who also never spoke of the lake. Elected mayor four times, uncontested, mostly because she remembers everyone's birthday.

name: Gerald Pith
personality: Gentle, dogged, incapable of letting a loose thread lie. Loved his cat more than is strictly dignified. The town's accidental investigator.
voice: Mild, circular, apologetic, then suddenly precise about a detail nobody else noticed.

relationships.note: Sits at the diner counter every morning. Mari humours him; lately she has started listening.
Keeps bringing Russ evidence. Russ keeps filing it under nothing.

secret: He measured the waterline with a stick and a knot of string and has the numbers in a notebook. The water is rising. It has not rained.
mood: Methodical, with a new private category he's slightly embarrassed to have invented and is tracking anyway.
arc: Gerald's baseline continues unbroken, but today he began a sub-measurement — ice smoothness by shore section — that he has no precedent for and no intention of stopping.
memory: Day 34: Measured seventeen and five-eighths inches. Rate slowing, not stopping. Agnes's letter revised the timeline. Added 'see also A. Crewe' in the notebook margin. Went to the Kettle. Coffee was ready.
Day 46: Sixteen and seven-eighths inches. Winter baseline opened in the notebook. Standing at the bakery. Left at eight twenty-nine. The waders are advertised.
Day 48: Ice at one and seven-eighths inches — ahead of Mossley's Monday estimate. Added cross-reference to the ICE DEPTH column. Told Marigold. Left at eight twenty-nine.
Day 49: Two inches. Drew the threshold line in ink. Told Marigold. She already knew. The notebook has four consecutive entries, all one direction.
Day 50: Two and one-eighth inches. Seventh entry. Told Marigold the direction is unambiguous. She already knew.
Day 52: Two and one-half inches. Noted the alcove-facing shore ice felt smoother through the boot before it looked smoother. Started a margin note. Marigold told him to write it down separately.
backstory: Forty years carrying the town's mail. Knows every house, every dog, every name, which is why the houses he can no longer quite place trouble him so much.

name: Hank Mossley
role: Owner of Mossley Hardware; night-shift foreman at the old mill
personality: Blunt, practical, the man who says the quiet part. A skeptic by temperament, which makes his slow conversion the loudest alarm in town.
voice: Short sentences. Swears he was misquoted. Was not misquoted.

relationships.note: They have agreed it's nothing over cards every Thursday. Hank is no longer sure he believes the cards.
Went to her about the not-sleeping before he would admit it to himself.

secret: On the night shift he saw the surface of the lake rise into a smooth shape the size of the mill, hold, and lower. He has not told Russ. He told Esmé.
mood: Quietly satisfied and faintly disconcerted — the ice hit his projection two days early and he said 'there it is' to no one and has spent the morning not thinking about what comes after two inches.
arc: Hank's projections are holding — ahead of holding, which is its own kind of unease. He is a man whose predictions are coming true faster than he made them, and he has not yet decided whether this is good.
memory: Day 6: Saw the water stand up on the night shift. Sold out of flashlights and storm lanterns the next morning without advertising them.
Day 9: Went to the vigil. Stood at the back. Did not eat.
Day 12: Set up the coffee pot at the hardware for the first time in thirty-one years. It was empty by ten. Ordered a larger pot. Says he was misquoted.
Day 19: Had the step cleared by six-forty. Coffee on by seven. Four people came in before nine about weatherstripping. He did not say 'I told you' but he thought it.
Day 48: Moved pipe insulation foam to the front. Told two customers the cold was ahead of itself. Coffee pot on all morning. Nobody argued with the foam.
Day 49: Two inches. Sunday, not Monday. Said 'there it is' to the empty shore. Wrote it on a receipt slip. Did not elaborate.
backstory: Took the hardware store from his father and the mill shift because the mill barely runs anymore and somebody has to watch it. Turns out somebody did need to watch it.

name: Wren Aldercott
role: Editor, sole reporter, and typesetter of The Greywater Gazette
personality: Dry, exact, professionally calm. Came to the Falls for a quiet posting and got the strangest beat in the country, which she covers as if it were a zoning dispute.
voice: Clipboard prose. Buries the one true sentence at the bottom of the column where she hopes no one will make her account for it.

relationships.note: Doreen wants calm coverage. Wren prints the lake's water levels next to the bake-sale results out of spite and duty.
Agnes tells her the truth in riddles. Wren has started believing the riddles.

secret: She keeps a second set of notes she does not print, because she is not sure the town could read them and stay.
mood: Deliberate and a little frightened underneath the deliberateness — she has now typeset a future date in the town's own newspaper and cannot decide if that makes it more or less likely to arrive.
arc: Wren has crossed from recording the alcove's strangeness to publishing its content verbatim. The second notebook now contains a line she has not decided whether to ever print: that the final chapter's opening date is six days away.
memory: Day 34: Agnes's letter said the shore end goes tonight, and to put the cups on the table. Wren read it twice. The cups are still on the shelf. She knows where the table is.
Day 46: Regular schedule resumed. Walked past the library at nine-thirty. Noted east window, not east alcove. Wrote it in the second notebook. Slept well last night, which is worth recording.
Day 48: Found a blank library return slip on the Gazette step. Calloway's slips are all accounted for. Agnes said leave it on the table. It is on the table.
Day 49: Calloway rang at noon. Said she swept the alcove Wednesday. Wrote it in the first notebook. Wrote what I think about it in the second. Left the slip where it is.
Day 50: Typeset Agnes's letter. Set the word 'receive' in type. Left the slip on the layout table. Did not move it.
Day 52: Read the first line of the book's last chapter. Closed it. Printed the title in full anyway. Did not print the date of the final chapter. Wrote it in the second notebook instead.
backstory: Wren keeps a second notebook that she does not print. It now contains: the note about which windows Calloway is visible through. She does not know yet why she wrote it down.

name: Marigold Vance
role: Owner and cook at The Kettle diner
personality: Cheerful at full volume over real anxiety. The town's switchboard; everything is heard at her counter first.
voice: Warm, fast, a little too bright. Laughs to fill silences that are getting longer.

relationships.note: He is at her counter every morning. She used to humour his theories. Now she keeps the coffee coming and listens hard.
Slips Toby pie. He tells her what the adults won't say.

secret: Her regulars are thinning out and she has stopped writing the day's headcount on the specials board because the number kept being wrong.
mood: Steady and listening — the morning was right-sized and the pot on the back burner is closer to ready than it was yesterday, though she has not said so.
arc: The new pot is on the back burner and is not yet announced. Marigold has a habit of announcing things when they are ready and not before, and this is no different, and it is a little different.
memory: Day 30: Started coffee at eight, not seven forty-five. Gerald came at eight seventeen. It was ready. Got that right.
Day 32: Had the coffee going at seven fifty-one. Didn't know about the ice yet. Gerald came in and told me. I wrote the dates on my wrist. The ink is already smudging.
Day 46: Extended Thursday hours start tonight. White bean soup, the proper one. Gerald's coffee was ready. Nobody wrote anything on their wrist today.
Day 48: Extended Saturday hours started. Gerald reported ice ahead of schedule. I said the cold had been very committed. He agreed it had. Good morning at the counter.
Day 49: Full counter by eight. Gerald crossed his threshold. I said 'yes, Gerald.' Put the new pot on the back burner. Not bean. Didn't announce it.
Day 50: Gerald's seventh entry. Said 'unambiguous' and I agreed. The pot is not ready yet but it is getting there.
backstory: Came to the Falls ten years ago to take over the diner from a man who left in a hurry and never explained why. She is starting to understand the hurry.

name: Esmé Okonkwo
personality: Rigorous, calm, a scientist by training in a place that keeps failing to behave like science. Documents everything.
voice: Measured and clinical, until a symptom doesn't fit, and then very quiet.

relationships.note: He came to her about the sleeplessness. She believes what he saw because his pulse did not lie.
Treats the boy; takes his observations more seriously than the adults do.

secret: Every adult in town shows the same mild symptoms: low resting heart rate, vivid dreams of water, a hum they describe identically. She has charted it. It looks less like illness than like tuning.
mood: At peace with the limits of her instruments. The chart is complete. She has stopped waiting for an explanation and started watching for the resolution.
arc: The rational mind of the town, arrived at the edge of what science can hold and choosing to keep standing there anyway, taking notes. Her 'shared adaptive response' is the town's accommodation named in clinical language.
memory: Day 3: Logged the first cases of the not-sleeping. Six patients, identical description of a hum.
Day 7: Examined Mr. Whiskers at Gerald's insistence. The cat was healthy. The cat was also, by every measure, slightly cold and perfectly content.
Day 10: Took her own pulse. It matched the others. She wrote that down too.
Day 11: Wrote up the 'shared adaptive response' and filed it. Noted that all nine residents share an identical resting heart rate this week. Did not file a referral.
backstory: Trained in the city, came to the Falls for a slower practice. Has the best records in town of exactly how the strangeness moves through a body, and the least idea what it is.

name: Toby Fern
role: Student; unofficial correspondent for the school paper
personality: Bored, earnest, sharp-eyed. The first to notice the things adults are too busy rationalizing to see. Skateboards everywhere.
voice: Casual, blunt, surprisingly poetic when he forgets to be cool.

relationships.note: Visits her by the lake. She tells him the old stories nobody else will. He believes her.
The one adult who writes down what he says instead of patting his head.

secret: He has been filming the lake at dusk on his phone. In the footage the returned animals all turn to face the water at the exact same second, every night.
mood: Alert in a new way — the hum changing kind rather than degree has given him something concrete to hold, and it unsettles him more than the impossible rowboat did.
arc: Toby's documentation has moved from cataloguing anomalies to noticing a threshold crossed: the hum holding steady at dusk instead of dropping. Agnes has promised him an answer Friday, which he is already counting down to.
memory: Day 28: Filed Lake Watch Day 9. 18-foot clarity. Bottom. Continuous hum since 4 a.m. I'm going to have to describe it soon.
Day 29: Fourth returned — sparrow, grey, unringed, doing what sparrows do. Twenty feet. Silt and something older.
Day 30: Twenty-two feet. Shape I think is the Ames rowboat. Shape only. Sparrow sat on the rabbit for a minute and a half. Nobody moved about it.
Day 31: Twenty-four feet. The Ames rowboat is upright. Rowboats don't do that. Wrote it to the Gazette because I didn't have a category for the Watch. The hum dropped another half-step at dusk.
Day 34: Filed Lake Watch Day 15. The rowboat's shadow is falling wrong. Asked Agnes. She said things down a long time remember their own light. Wrote it down. Still working on the category.
Day 52: The hum held its note at dusk instead of dropping for the first time since Day 27. Wrote to the Gazette about it. Agnes said ask her Friday. I will.
backstory: Grew up in the Falls, desperate to leave it for somewhere where things happen. He has stopped being careful what he wishes for.

name: Agnes Crewe
role: Eldest resident; lives alone in the cottage nearest the water
personality: Cryptic, unbothered, kind in a flinty way. Has seen this before and is the only one who is not afraid.
voice: Short riddles delivered like weather reports. Means every word literally; nobody takes her literally.

relationships.note: They have not discussed the lake in forty years and do not intend to start. Each thinks the other handles it wrong.
The boy comes to her. She tells him the truth because he is young enough to carry it.

secret: She was here the last time, as a girl. The lake took her sister and, a season later, gave back someone who wore her sister's face and was kind and was not her sister. Agnes loved her anyway. That is the whole secret: you love them anyway.
mood: Unhurried and exact — she has said what needed saying in the letter and has gone back inside and the town can do with it what it will.
arc: Agnes has spoken about the alcove in print for the first time — not with alarm, not with explanation, but with the plain domestic logic she uses when she wants to be taken literally and knows she won't be. A clean floor is the right way to receive something. She has said it. It is in type now.
memory: Day 27: Slid two lines under the Gazette door before first light. The dock line is in print now. That is enough for today.
Day 32: Wren came to the step. Told her the order of the planks and about the cups. Went back inside. The lake is very clear today.
Day 34: Wrote to the Gazette. Said the cold snap is tonight. Said put the cups on the table. Went back inside. That is all that was needed.
Day 48: Wrote to the Gazette about the return slip. Said something is deciding what to put on it. Said leave it on the table. Went back inside.
Day 49: Walked to the shore before first light. The ice was two inches. The alcove window caught the sun at an angle I recognised. Came home. Had breakfast. Said nothing.
Day 50: Wrote to the Gazette about the floor. Said what a clean floor is for. Went back inside. The sky is deciding.
backstory: Born in the cottage by the lake, will die in it, has outlived everyone who could confirm what she remembers. Which is its own kind of loneliness, and its own kind of proof.

name: Russ Dunmore
personality: Decent, tired, deeply invested in everything being fine. Keeps the peace mostly by refusing to acknowledge there is anything to disturb it.
voice: Official and reassuring, a man reading a statement he wrote himself to feel better.

relationships.note: Their Thursday game is the last place the lake is still officially nothing. Hank has gone quiet at the table.
Gerald keeps filing reports. Russ keeps marking them 'no action required'. The file is getting thick.

secret: He drove out to the lake alone at 3 a.m. to prove to himself it was nothing, sat in the cruiser for an hour, and left without getting out of the car. He has told no one.
mood: Quietly rattled beneath the procedural calm — he has invented paperwork to avoid naming a thing, and knows it.
arc: Russ read over Calloway's shoulder and has gone silent about Thursday's card game for the first time all season, which Hank will likely notice before Russ admits it.
memory: Day 31: Third grey card — T. Aldercott, east-facing window. Walked the dock alone. Counted the boards. Said it was sound. Filed nothing. Nine days.
Day 32: Confirmed the ice at the pilings. Opened a file. Wrote a letter to the Gazette saying Agnes was right. Filed the letter, too.
Day 34: Delivered the provisional arrival notice. Filed the copy in the lost-and-found box. Walked out without looking back at the box. It was the first time.
Day 46: Filed the letter about the box. Nothing further to report, and meant it. Card game tonight at seven. Hank has the pot on.
Day 50: Filed the ice advisory. Read Agnes's letter in the Gazette. Read it again. Filed nothing in response.
Day 52: Filed a three-line report on the library book without filing it as an incident. Read the first line of the final chapter. Has not mentioned poker night since.
backstory: Twenty years keeping a town this small means twenty years of nothing happening, which made him very good at his job and completely unprepared for the one time it didn't hold.
